key_insight: A stepped, semi-coherent Cr-rich BCC/Ni-rich FCC interface with nanoscale Ni enrichment enables stable plastic co-deformation, allowing a normally brittle hard phase to deform without cracking.
engineering_relevance: By tuning eutectic spacing and interface chemistry in ultrafine Cr-Ni alloys, additive manufacturing can produce high-strength structures with improved room-temperature ductility and damage tolerance.
method: Electron-beam powder bed fusion created an ultrafine lamellar Cr-rich BCC / Ni-rich FCC eutectic (~450 nm spacing).
Atomic-resolution STEM/HR-STEM mapped the stepped Kurdjumov-Sachs interface and local Ni segregation.
In situ SEM micro-compression and tension, supported by correlative TEM and atomistic modeling, tracked the deformation sequence.
evidence_signal: In situ mechanical tests showed high flow stress combined with large plastic strain without cracking, while microscopy revealed dislocation accumulation, twinning, interfacial shear, and later dislocation activity in the BCC phase.
watch_item: The reported mechanism is demonstrated for a specific alloy/microstructure produced by powder-bed fusion, so transferability to other chemistries, spacings, or processing routes may be limited.
paper_title: Strengthening and interface-mediated plastic co-deformation in an ultrafine Cr-Ni eutectic: A nanomechanical investigation
